CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.

CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORKS In this paper, we presented quantifying multi-aspect news sentiment with considered time sequence data and then forecasting stock prices next days using sentiment and technical indicators. In feature extraction, a daily confidence score from the classification model is used to quantify sentiment. The active learning model produced a monthly classification model to represent model knowledge. The Transformer Encoder Gated Recurrent Unit (TEGRU) out- performed another Transformer architecture for stock price forecasting on eight stock issuers. The TEGRU model consists of the transformer encoder to learn time series pattern data with multi-head attention

CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.

III. MATERIALS AND METHOD III. MATERIALS AND METHOD The architecture proposed in this study was validated on the DEAP, public dataset. Considering the nature of the dataset, where samples that belong to different severity levels share common features, a multitask vector label representation along with severity-oriented decoding is proposed. title_content: Utilization of Pretrained Weights in Medical Image Analysis Studies

CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.

The choice between one-stage and two-stage object detec- tion models depends heavily on the specific demands of the task, particularly in medical imaging contexts such as colorectal cancer (CRC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), gastric lesions, and polypoid lesions. One-stage detectors, such as YOLO and SSD, excel in speed and computational efficiency, making them ideal for real-time applications. For instance, YOLOv5 demonstrated remarkable performance in CRC detection with nearly perfect metrics—precision (99.071%), recall (98.064%), and mAP (98.8%)—allowing clinicians to analyze colonoscopy videos efficiently during live procedures . SSD also performed well in CRC detection, achieving precision (90.42%) and recall (91.32%), though its mAP (87.5%) was slightly lower than YOLO-
title_content: Comparison of One-stage Detectors in Medical Imaging: Application to Colorectal Cancer Detection
referensi_citation: The choice between one-stage and two-stage object detection models for medical imaging tasks, such as colorectal cancer (CRC) detection, hinges on specific task demands. YOLOv5 demonstrated superior performance with precision of 99.071%, recall of 98.064%, and mAP of 98.8% in real-time CRC analysis during colonoscopy procedures, outperforming SSD's metrics (precision: 90.42%, recall: 91.32%, mAP: 87.5%).
